name: MAGMA (Multi-Graph based Agentic Memory Architecture)
maintainer: Dongming Jiang, Yi Li, Guanpeng Li, Bingzhe Li — affiliations: Department of Computer Science, University of Texas at Dallas; Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, University of Florida
last_major_release: [uncertain] arXiv v1 submitted 2026-01-06; v2 released 2026-04-16; no formal version tags in repository
activity: [uncertain] GitHub repository FredJiang0324/MAMGA; early research release; star count and contributor count not confirmed as of 2026-04
storage_backend: Hybrid: in-memory graph structures (NetworkX or equivalent) for multi-graph representation + vector database (FAISS or similar) for semantic similarity search. Implementation files: graph_db.py (graph layer) and vector_db.py (vector layer). No Neo4j or Postgres dependency described.
representation_primitive: entity+edge, multi-graph. Each memory item (conversation event or knowledge unit) is represented as a node indexed across four orthogonal relational graphs simultaneously: (1) Semantic graph — conceptual similarity edges between memory nodes; (2) Temporal graph — immutable timeline of events with chronological ordering edges; (3) Causal graph — logical entailment / cause-effect edges ('why' relationships); (4) Entity graph — tracks people, places, and things across time, solving object-permanence continuity. This disentangled multi-graph representation allows each memory to appear in multiple relational contexts without data duplication.
api_shape: Python library. Code at https://github.com/FredJiang0324/MAMGA. No first-party MCP server. No REST API described.
hosting: Self-host only. Research code release; no managed cloud or SaaS offering.
llm_dependency: Both ingest and retrieval. Ingest: LLM used via a dual-stream mechanism — foreground stream handles fast event ingestion with minimal LLM overhead; background asynchronous stream performs structural consolidation (entity extraction, causal/semantic edge inference) using LLM. Retrieval: LLM backbone (gpt-4o-mini, temperature=0.0) used for query analysis (intent classification) and context synthesis. LLM cannot be disabled.
schema_extensibility: prescribed+learned hybrid. Four graph types (semantic, temporal, causal, entity) are prescribed architectural components. Within each graph, edge weights and new entity nodes are inferred by LLM during background consolidation, constituting a form of online learned extension. The four-graph split is fixed; adding a fifth graph type requires code changes.
schema_richness: 4 prescribed graph types (semantic, temporal, causal, entity) each with their own edge semantics: SEMANTIC_SIMILAR, PRECEDES/FOLLOWS (temporal), CAUSES/CAUSED_BY (causal), MENTIONS/REFERENCED_BY (entity). All memory items are nodes shared across graphs. Multi-label: yes — each node appears in up to all four graphs simultaneously. Multi-graph: yes — this is MAGMA's defining architectural contribution. Compared to Gimle §5 target (20+ entities / 15+ edges): MAGMA's node types are event-centric (conversation episodes) not domain-specific; it does not model File/Symbol/Module/APIEndpoint/UIComponent entities. The 4-axis multi-graph design is relevant but the entity vocabulary does not overlap with Gimle §5.
temporal_model: event-log with dedicated temporal graph. The temporal graph creates an immutable ordered timeline of events with PRECEDES/FOLLOWS edges. This is stronger than session-ordering but not a formal bi-temporal model — there is no recorded_at / valid_from / valid_to distinction. The temporal graph ensures 'when' queries traverse chronological edges, not just semantic similarity.
forgetting_policy: none described. No LRU, semantic-dedup, or eviction policy. The multi-graph grows monotonically. Background consolidation may merge duplicate entities implicitly but no explicit forgetting mechanism is specified.
ingest_latency_class: real-time-incremental (foreground) + batch-offline (background). Dual-stream architecture: the foreground stream ingests events with sub-second latency (minimal processing); the background asynchronous stream performs heavier LLM-driven structural consolidation (edge inference, entity merging) without blocking the foreground. This yields 40% lower query latency compared to single-stream baselines.
retrieval_modes: graph-traversal (policy-guided multi-graph) + vector search (semantic graph anchoring). Retrieval flow: (1) Query Analysis classifies intent and identifies anchor nodes via vector search over semantic graph; (2) Adaptive Traversal Policy routes to specific graph views — causal graph for 'why' queries, temporal graph for 'when' queries, entity graph for 'who/what' queries — with configurable traversal depth (default max 5 hops); (3) Context synthesis assembles retrieved subgraph into structured context. Parameters: top-20 vector candidates, keyword threshold 0.3, top-5 final context nodes.
retrieval_agenticity: policy-guided traversal. MAGMA's Adaptive Traversal Policy is the most sophisticated retrieval agenticity in this survey: intent classification determines which graph view(s) to traverse, pruning irrelevant subgraphs before expansion. This is beyond multi-hop (fixed depth traversal) — it is query-type-conditional graph routing. Closer to RL-policy in spirit, though implemented as a rule-based classifier in the current version.
retrieval_budget_control: yes. First-class retrieval parameters: vector_top_k=20, keyword_threshold=0.3, context_top_n=5, max_traversal_depth=5. These are configurable per-query, giving explicit control over retrieval cost/latency.
multi_project_namespace: none described. Single-agent memory system. No multi-project partitioning or cross-project query support.
multi_user_access: none. Single-agent model. No RBAC or provenance-aware sharing.
embedder_options: Two options: (1) Default: all-MiniLM-L6-v2 (sentence-transformers), 384-dimensional vectors, fully local, no API cost. (2) Optional: OpenAI text-embedding-3-small, 1536-dimensional vectors. Custom embedder support implied by the modular vector_db.py layer.
llm_client_options: gpt-4o-mini (default, temperature=0.0 for deterministic reasoning). OpenAI API client. No Anthropic or Ollama adapters described in the paper. LLM required for query analysis and background consolidation; cannot be disabled.
cost_privacy: Token cost at ingest (foreground): near-zero LLM calls — fast event ingestion with minimal processing. Token cost at ingest (background): moderate — LLM calls for entity extraction, causal/semantic edge inference per consolidation batch. Token cost per query: moderate — one LLM call for intent classification + one for synthesis. On LongMemEval: 95% token reduction vs full-context baseline (3.37k tokens per query vs ~67k for full-context), query latency 1.47s (40% faster than next-best baseline A-MEM). Data leaves host when using OpenAI APIs for embeddings or LLM calls.
code_artifact_linkage: none. MAGMA is a conversational/episodic memory system. No code-artifact, file-path, symbol, or repository modeling. Not applicable to code understanding contexts.
code_analysis_features: none. No call graph, diff-awareness, symbol index, or code-specific analysis. Purely episodic/conversational domain.
memory_os_layer: plaintext (external memory). Operates at the external-storage tier — the four graphs and vector store are persistent data structures outside model weights. No parametric or activation (KV-cache) tier. Cross-type migration not supported.
self_improvement_mechanism: online-alias-learning / LLM-reflection (partial). The background dual-stream consolidation process is a form of LLM-reflection: it asynchronously re-processes ingested events to infer causal and semantic edges not available during fast foreground ingestion. This yields progressively richer graph structure over time. Not fully RL-policy-driven but more sophisticated than manual curation.
provenance_standard: none formally. Causal graph edges provide implicit causal provenance (CAUSES/CAUSED_BY chain). Temporal graph preserves event ordering. No W3C-PROV, OpenTelemetry, or MCP-native trace emitted.
benchmark_results: LongMemEval: 61.2% average accuracy; 95% token reduction vs full-context baseline (3.37k tokens/query); query latency 1.47s (40% faster than next-best baseline A-MEM). LoCoMo: 0.7 overall judge score; outperforms baselines by 18.6% to 45.5%. Both benchmarks use gpt-4o-mini backbone. Version 2 (2026-04-16) adds ultra-long context evaluation (100k+ token conversations). No SWE-bench, CrossCodeEval, or HotpotQA results reported.
strengths: 1. Disentangled multi-graph representation: separating semantic, temporal, causal, and entity relations into orthogonal graphs enables query-type-conditional traversal that is fundamentally impossible in a single-graph or embedding-only store. The causal graph uniquely addresses 'why' reasoning gaps in existing systems. 2. Dual-stream ingest with 40% latency and 95% token reduction: the background consolidation architecture achieves high structural richness without blocking foreground responsiveness — directly relevant to Gimle's need for real-time agent activity ingest. 3. First-class retrieval budget control: configurable top_k, keyword_threshold, traversal_depth parameters give explicit cost/latency control per query, enabling Gimle to tune retrieval for different agent workload profiles.
weaknesses: 1. Episodic/conversational schema only: MAGMA's node vocabulary is event-centric (conversation episodes); it does not model code artifacts, project entities, decisions, or iterations. Substantial schema work required to apply to Gimle §5. 2. No bi-temporal model: despite a dedicated temporal graph, there is no valid_from/to + recorded_at distinction; the temporal graph tracks event order, not valid-time vs transaction-time. Gimle §5's bi-temporal requirement is not met. 3. GPT-4o-mini dependency for consolidation: the background LLM consolidation stream cannot be disabled; this creates ongoing API cost and a privacy surface for any sensitive repository content processed.
fit_with_gimle_section_5: Verdict: steal-specific-ideas. MAGMA's multi-graph architecture is the most directly relevant design pattern for Gimle's N+1 Graphiti schema. Specific ideas: (1) 4-axis multi-label graph decomposition — Gimle §5 targets a 'faceted 4-axis multi-label' design. MAGMA validates this approach empirically: separating semantic, temporal, causal, and entity dimensions into distinct edge-type namespaces outperforms a unified multi-label graph. For Gimle, this translates to: :SEMANTICALLY_RELATED edges on the semantic axis; :PRECEDES/:FOLLOWS on the temporal axis; :CAUSED_BY/:INFLUENCES on the causal/decision axis; :REFERENCES/:USES on the structural/entity axis. (2) Adaptive Traversal Policy — Gimle's retrieval layer should implement query-intent classification to route agent queries to the appropriate graph axis. A 'what changed in file X?' query should prioritize temporal edges; a 'why was this decision made?' query should traverse causal edges; a 'where is this symbol used?' query should traverse entity/structural edges. Example policy sketch: IF query.intent == 'temporal' THEN traverse [:PRECEDES/:FOLLOWS] from anchor; IF query.intent == 'causal' THEN traverse [:CAUSED_BY/:INFLUENCES] from anchor. (3) Dual-stream ingest — Gimle should adopt a foreground (fast, minimal processing) + background (async LLM-driven structural enrichment) ingest pattern. New agent activities are immediately available for query via the foreground stream; richer causal and semantic edges are added asynchronously by the background consolidation process without blocking agent execution.
